_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Universal Care: Functional Needs. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Universal Care: Functional Needs

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Universal Care: Functional Needs
points: To meet and maintain the additional support required for patients with functional needs during the delivery of prehospital care

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Patients who are identified by the World Health Organization's International Classification of Functioning, Disability, and Health that have experienced a decrement in health resulting in some degree of disability. According to the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, this includes, but is not limited to, individuals with physical, sensory, mental health, and cognitive and/or intellectual disabilities affecting their ability to function independently without assistance
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
None noted

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Scene safety and PPE; primary survey (ABCDE)
Oxygen via NRM targeting SpO2 94-98% if indicated
Position of comfort; hemorrhage control as needed
CPR/AED if patient becomes pulseless
Epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis if applicable
Naloxone intranasal unit-dose for suspected opioid involvement

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Blood glucose monitoring; oral glucose if hypoglycemic and alert
12-lead ECG acquisition and transmission
CPAP for respiratory distress
Aspirin for suspected ischemic chest pain
Albuterol SVN for bronchospasm component
IV access maintenance (non-medicated)

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV/IO access initiation
IV/IO fluid resuscitation as indicated
Waveform capnography
IV analgesics (fentanyl, morphine) per pain management protocol
IV antiemetics (ondansetron) per protocol
IV D50W/D10W for hypoglycemia

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: 12-lead ECG interpretation
Endotracheal intubation for airway compromise
RSI per protocol
Advanced antidysrhythmic or hemodynamic support per specific protocol
All parenteral medications per protocol
Vasopressors for refractory hemodynamic compromise

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: For patients with communication barriers (language or sensory), it may be desirable to obtain secondary confirmation of pertinent data (e.g., allergies) from the patient's family, interpreters, or written or electronic medical records. The family members can be an excellent source of information and the presence of a family member can have a calming influence on some of these patients

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Communication Barriers a. Language Barriers: i. Expressive and/or receptive aphasia
Medical translation cards
Telephone-accessible services with live language interpreters
Methods through which the patient augments his/her communication skills (e.g., eye blinking, nodding) should be noted, utilized as able, and communicated to the receiving facility
Electronic applications for translation
Sign language
Lip reading
Hearing aids

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Document all barriers in the NEMSIS element 'eHistory.01 ' Barriers to Patient Care' (NEMSIS Required National Element)
' Document specific physical barriers in the appropriate exam elements (e.g., 'blind' under Eye Assessment; or paralysis, weakness, or speech problems under Neurological Assessment)
' Document any of the following, as appropriate in the narrative: o Language barriers: . The patient's primary language of fluency
The identification of the person assisting with the communication
The methods through which the patient augments his/her communication skills
o Sensory barriers: . The methods through which the patient augments his/her communication skills

Written communication between the patient and the EMS professional is part of the medical record, even if it is on a scrap sheet of paper, and it should be retained with the same collation, storage, and confidentiality policies and procedures that are applicable to the written or electronic patient care report
o Assistance adjuncts (devices that facilitate the activities of life for the patient)

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Contact medical direction for clinical deterioration or interventions outside standard scope
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
